Title: The Gram-positive bacterium Romboutsia ilealis harbors a polysaccharide synthase that can produce (1,3;1,4)-β-D-glucans

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glycosyltransferase family 2 (GT2) polysaccharide synthase with D,D,D,Q(Q/R)XRW motifs and C-terminal PilZ domain
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Likely contributes to bacterial exopolysaccharide architecture, biofilm formation, autoaggregation, cell-to-surface interactions, and potential biomaterial applications
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Exopolysaccharides (EPS) of R. ilealis; alcohol-insoluble residue/EPS fraction and cytoplasm-associated signal in transgenic yeast
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RiGT2 is sufficient to drive synthesis and secretion/accumulation of (1,3;1,4)-β-D-glucan; expression in yeast produced predominantly DP3-rich glucan with DP3/DP4 ~15:1
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RiGT2 was the only GT2 candidate in R. ilealis CRIBT carrying the canonical polysaccharide synthase signature and was nominated as the (1,3;1,4)-β-D-glucan synthase candidate. 2. Heterologous expression of RiGT2 in Saccharomyces cerevisiae LoGSA produced lichenase-releasable DP3 and minor DP4 oligosaccharides, demonstrating direct gain-of-function synthesis of (1,3;1,4)-β-D-glucan. 3. Microsomal fractions containing RiGT2 incorporated radiolabeled glucose from UDP-[14C]Glc into alcohol-insoluble glucan polymers that were degraded by lichenase, confirming enzymatic activity in vitro. 4. Deletion of the C-terminal PilZ domain abolished glucan synthesis, indicating that the PilZ domain is essential for activity even though c-di-GMP binding was not detected.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: (1,3;1,4)-β-D-glucan polymer yielding DP3 (G4G3G) and DP4 (G4G4G3G) after lichenase digestion
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes glucose from UDP-glucose into an unbranched mixed-linkage (1,3;1,4)-β-D-glucan containing both β-1,4 and β-1,3 linkages
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step for product formation; direct synthase of the target polymer
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Membrane; microsomal fraction; transmembrane polysaccharide synthase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected upon galactose induction in transgenic LoGSA yeast
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Galactose induction in yeast; in vitro assays with Mg2+, UDP-glucose, lichenase digestion; c-di-GMP supplementation tested with no activation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Direct gain-of-function heterologous expression in S. cerevisiae; lichenase oligosaccharide profiling by HPAEC-PAD and MALDI-TOF MS; linkage analysis; immunofluorescence/immunogold microscopy; radiometric in vitro enzyme assay; PilZ-domain deletion analysis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Bioinformatic database mining from CAZy and NCBI; no accessioned omics dataset reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: RiGT2-derived glucan in yeast had a much higher DP3/DP4 ratio (15:1) than native R. ilealis EPS glucan (2:1). Structural modeling suggested similarity to bacterial cellulose synthase BcsA and plant HvCslF6. RiGT2 activity was independent of added c-di-GMP, and isolated PilZ domain showed no detectable c-di-GMP binding by ITC or SPR, yet the PilZ domain was required for activity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Heterologous product structure differs from native bacterial EPS, suggesting host membrane environment or C-terminal GFP fusion may alter product profile
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful enzyme for engineering microbial or synthetic platforms to produce tailored mixed-linkage β-glucans with desired DP3/DP4 ratios for biomaterial or health-related applications; PilZ domain integrity should be preserved during engineering
